content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to creating inclusive learning environments where every student—regardless of linguistic background, learning profile, or ability—can access a world-class Cambridge education while meeting Indonesian national requirements. Our inclusion model recognizes that:

items: Diversity is an asset: Students with varied linguistic, cultural, cognitive, and socio-economic backgrounds enrich our learning communities
All students can learn: With appropriate support, scaffolding, and high expectations, every learner can make meaningful progress
Early intervention prevents failure: Proactive identification and tiered support systems reduce the need for intensive remediation
Inclusion is whole-school responsibility: Every teacher is a teacher of EAL learners and students with diverse needs
content.title: 8.1.1 Vision for Inclusive Education

items: CIS/WASC Standard E: "The school is committed to diversity, equity, inclusion, and justice"
Cambridge Policy B6.2: "Centres must make reasonable adjustments for candidates with access requirements"
Indonesian Law No. 8/2016: Guarantees educational rights for students with disabilities
SLO 2 (Communication) and SLO 3 (Global Competence): As outlined in Section 1.3

content.text: Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) and Response to Intervention (RTI) Eduversal Partner Schools implement a comprehensive MTSS/RTI framework that provides:

steps: Universal supports (Tier 1) for all students
Targeted interventions (Tier 2) for some students
Intensive supports (Tier 3) for few students

content.text: This framework integrates academic and behavioral interventions with social-emotional support.
Eduversal implements a three-tiered MTSS/RTI model that provides increasingly intensive support based on student need: Key Principles:

items: Data-driven decision making: Use EASE benchmark data, Cambridge Checkpoints, teacher assessments, and language proficiency measures
Fluid movement between tiers: Students move up/down based on response to intervention
Prevention focus: Tier 1 quality teaching reduces need for higher-tier support
Collaborative model: Learning Support Team coordinates with classroom teachers, subject leaders, and families
content.title: 8.1.2 Multi-Tiered System of Supports (MTSS) Framework

subsections.title: TIER 1: UNIVERSAL AND QUALITY FIRST TEACHING

content.text: All teachers provide Quality First Teaching that meets the needs of 80-85% of students without additional intervention. This includes: Differentiation by:

items: Content: Providing texts/materials at varied complexity levels
Process: Offering multiple pathways to learning (visual, auditory, kinesthetic)
Product: Allowing students to demonstrate learning in varied formats
Environment: Flexible seating, visual supports, reduced distractions
content.title: 8.2.1 Core Instructional Expectations

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 5.7 for detailed differentiation strategies and Section 7.3.4 for differentiation in lesson observations. Universal Design for Learning (UDL) Principles:

items: Multiple means of representation: Visual aids, graphic organizers, bilingual glossaries
Multiple means of engagement: Choice, real-world relevance, culturally responsive examples
Multiple means of action/expression: Written, oral, visual, or digital assessments
Pre-teach academic vocabulary with visual support
Use sentence frames and language scaffolds
Provide think-pair-share time before whole-class discussion
Display key vocabulary and success criteria visibly
Allow use of translation tools/bilingual dictionaries strategically

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 5.8.3 for EAL instructional strategies across Cambridge subjects.

items: Week 1-2 of school year: EAL proficiency assessment (all new students), EASE baseline (Grades 3-12)
Week 4: Teacher referral window opens for students not responding to Tier 1
3x per year: EASE benchmarks (September, January, April) identify students below grade-level expectations
content.title: 8.2.2 Tier 1 Screening and Progress Monitoring

content.text: Tier 1 Data Sources: Red Flags for Tier 2 Referral:

items: EASE score \<40th percentile for grade level
Limited progress despite differentiated Tier 1 support (less than expected growth over 6-8 weeks)
Teacher concern about reading fluency, comprehension, or mathematical reasoning
Social-emotional struggles impacting learning
EAL proficiency stagnation (see Section 8.4.5)

steps: Teacher Referral (Week 4+ of term)
content.title: 8.3.1 Tier 2 Identification Process

items: Teacher completes SST Referral Form documenting:
Specific area(s) of concern (literacy, numeracy, behavior, language)
Tier 1 strategies already attempted
Current performance data (EASE, assessments, work samples)

steps: SST Meeting (within 2 weeks of referral)

items: Attendees: Classroom teacher, Year Level Coordinator, Learning Support Coordinator, EAL Coordinator (if applicable)
Outcome: Determine if Tier 2 intervention needed or if enhanced Tier 1 sufficient
Documented in: EduNav SST module

steps: Parent Communication (within 1 week of SST decision)

items: Principal/YLC informs parents of support plan
Document parental consent for intervention (required for Indonesian compliance)

steps: Intervention Begins (within 1 week of parent notification)

items: Group size: 3-6 students with similar needs
Frequency: 3-4x per week, 30 minutes per session
Duration: 8-12 week intervention cycle
Delivered by: Learning Support Teacher, trained Teaching Assistant, or specialist
Focus areas:
Phonics/decoding (Grades 1-3)
Reading fluency and comprehension (Grades 3-6)
Academic vocabulary and writing skills (Grades 6-8)
Progress monitoring: Every 2 weeks (oral reading fluency probes, comprehension checks)
content.title: 8.3.2 Tier 2 Intervention Models

items: Group size: 4-6 students
Frequency: 3x per week, 30 minutes
Duration: 8-12 week cycles
Focus areas:
Number sense and place value
Multiplication/division fluency
Fractions and proportional reasoning
Problem-solving strategies
Progress monitoring: Every 2 weeks (curriculum-based math probes)
For students: CEFR A2-B1 level requiring targeted language development beyond Tier 1 scaffolding
Models:
Push-in: EAL teacher co-teaches or works with small group during core lessons
Pull-out: Small group EAL lessons 2-3x per week focusing on academic language
Duration: Ongoing until proficiency exit criteria met (see 8.4.5)
See Section 8.4 for full EAL framework
Check-in/Check-out (CICO): Daily touchpoints with mentor teacher
Social skills groups: 1x per week, 45 minutes (e.g., friendship skills, emotional regulation)
Behavior contracts: For students with specific behavioral goals
Collaboration with: School counselor/psychologist
Every 2 weeks: Intervention-specific probes (reading fluency, math facts, etc.)
Every 6-8 weeks: Review at SST meeting
content.title: 8.3.3 Tier 2 Progress Monitoring and Exit Criteria

content.text: Decision Rules (after 6-8 week cycle): Exit Criteria from Tier 2:

items: EASE score improves to 40th percentile or above
Intervention-specific goals met (e.g., reading fluency at grade level)
Teacher confidence that student can succeed with Tier 1 differentiation only
Monitoring plan: Continue to track for 6-8 weeks post-exit to ensure sustained progress

content.text: Typical EAL Profile at Eduversal Schools:

items: 40-70% of students are EAL learners (varies by school)
Background: Indonesian L1 speakers; some with regional languages (Javanese, Sundanese, Betawi)
Exposure to English: Ranges from minimal (new to international school) to advanced (been in Cambridge system since Primary)
Challenge: Academic English proficiency often lags behind social/conversational English
content.title: 8.4.1 EAL Population and Context

content.text: Key Principle: EAL is not a learning difficulty—it is a developmental stage requiring explicit language instruction alongside content learning.
Eduversal uses a 5-stage EAL framework aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR): Alignment with Cambridge and WIDA:

items: Cambridge IGCSE ESL exam levels: Core (A2-B1), Extended (B1-B2)
WIDA English Language Development Standards: Used for supplementary proficiency assessment in some schools
content.title: 8.4.2 EAL Proficiency Framework (CEFR-Aligned)

steps: Pre-Enrollment Screening (Admissions)
content.title: 8.4.3 EAL Placement and Assessment Process

items: Parent questionnaire: languages spoken at home, prior schooling, English exposure
If EAL suspected: Flag for EAL assessment during first week

steps: EAL Proficiency Assessment (Week 1-2)

items: Tool: Eduversal EAL Screener (based on WIDA MODEL, Cambridge Placement Test, or similar)
Assesses: Listening, speaking, reading, writing
Administered by: EAL Coordinator or trained teacher
Documented in: EduNav student profile

steps: EAL Stage Designation

items: Student assigned to Stage 1-5 based on assessment
If Stage 1-2: Automatic Tier 3 EAL support
If Stage 3: Tier 2 EAL support
If Stage 4-5: Tier 1 with monitoring

steps: Support Plan Communicated

items: Parents notified of EAL stage and support plan within 1 week
Documented in student's Individual Learning Plan (if Tier 2/3)
Formative: Teacher observation, language-focused rubrics, oral language samples
Summative: EASE English benchmarks, Cambridge Checkpoint English, EAL proficiency re-assessment (annually or when exit criteria approached)
Differentiated instruction as per Section 5.8.3
Vocabulary pre-teaching, visual supports, sentence frames
No pull-out required
content.title: 8.4.4 EAL Instructional Models

content.text: Tier 2 (Stage 3 students): Option A: Push-In Model

items: EAL teacher co-teaches in mainstream classes (e.g., English, Science, Humanities)
Provides scaffolding, reteaching, and small-group support during lesson
Frequency: 2-4 periods per week
Students withdrawn from non-core subjects (e.g., electives, study hall) for targeted EAL lessons
Focus on academic vocabulary, writing skills, reading comprehension strategies
Frequency: 3-5 periods per week
Curriculum: Adapted from Cambridge Primary/Lower Secondary English \+ EAL-specific resources
Grades 1-5: Students spend 50-70% of day in EAL-designated classes with bilingual support
Modified curriculum in English, Math, Science
Indonesian national subjects taught in Bahasa Indonesia
Gradual mainstreaming into core subjects as proficiency improves
Grades 6-12:
Daily EAL pull-out lessons (5+ periods/week)
Modified assignments in mainstream classes
Bilingual Teaching Assistant support in core subjects
May substitute IGCSE First Language English with IGCSE English as a Second Language (ESL) or defer IGCSE entry
Use of Indonesian to explain complex concepts (especially Science, Math)
Bilingual dictionaries and glossaries provided
Translation of key instructions/assessments (sparingly, to build independence)

content.text: Exit from EAL Support (Tier 2 or 3 to Tier 1): Students may exit EAL services when they meet ALL of the following criteria:

steps: Proficiency Assessment: Score B1 or above (CEFR) on comprehensive EAL assessment
Academic Performance:
content.title: 8.4.5 EAL Exit Criteria and Monitoring

items: EASE English score at or above 40th percentile for grade level
Teacher evidence of success in mainstream classes without modifications

steps: Teacher Recommendation: Subject teachers confirm student can access curriculum independently
Observation Period: Sustained progress over 2 consecutive terms

items: Former EAL students monitored for 4 terms after exiting support
If student struggles, can re-enter Tier 2 EAL support
Documented in EduNav

content.text: Note: Students may exit EAL support but still receive Tier 1 differentiation (vocabulary scaffolding, extended time on assessments) as needed.

items: Made minimal/no progress in Tier 2 despite 12+ weeks of intervention
Have significant learning difficulties requiring individualized programming
Are Stage 1-2 EAL requiring intensive language acquisition support
Present with suspected special educational needs (SEN)
content.title: 8.5.1 Tier 3 Identification and Evaluation

steps: Tier 2 Review (SST Meeting)

items: Document lack of response to Tier 2 intervention
Rule out instructional/environmental factors (attendance, trauma, inadequate Tier 1/2 implementation)

steps: Comprehensive Evaluation (if SEN suspected)

items: Internal assessment by Learning Support Coordinator:
Cognitive/academic skills assessment (e.g., WIAT, KeyMath, reading inventories)
Behavioral/emotional screening (e.g., BASC, Conners)
Learning profile analysis
External assessment (if needed):
Educational psychologist (for suspected dyslexia, dyscalculia, ADHD, autism, intellectual disability)
Speech-language pathologist (for language disorders beyond EAL)
Occupational therapist (for fine motor/sensory needs)
Note: Many Eduversal schools partner with Jakarta-based clinics or private practitioners for specialized assessments

steps: Parent Consent and Collaboration

items: Written parental consent required for comprehensive evaluation (Indonesian law)
Parents provided with evaluation timeline and cost information (external assessments may require family contribution)
Regular communication throughout process

steps: Case Conference (within 4 weeks of evaluation completion)

items: Attendees: Principal, Learning Support Coordinator, teachers, parents, external specialists (if applicable)
Outcome: Determine eligibility for SEN support and develop IEP/ILP

content.text: SEN Identified in Eduversal Schools: A. Specific Learning Differences (SpLD):

items: Dyslexia: Difficulty with reading accuracy, fluency, and spelling despite adequate instruction
Dyscalculia: Difficulty with number sense, calculation, and mathematical reasoning
Dysgraphia: Difficulty with handwriting, written expression, and organizing thoughts on paper
Dyspraxia (DCD): Difficulty with motor planning and coordination
content.title: 8.5.2 Special Educational Needs (SEN) Categories

items: ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder): Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity impacting learning
Speech/language impairment (beyond EAL): Articulation, expressive/receptive language disorder
Note: Distinguish from EAL—SEN diagnosis requires difficulty in both L1 and L2
Challenges with social communication, sensory processing, and/or restricted/repetitive behaviors
Visual impairment, hearing impairment, physical disabilities

content.text: F. Social, Emotional, and Mental Health:

items: Anxiety, depression, trauma, or other mental health needs impacting learning
Significant limitations in cognitive functioning and adaptive behavior (rare in Eduversal schools due to admissions criteria, but occasional cases)
Eduversal schools assess each prospective student's fit individually
Schools are not required to admit students whose needs exceed available resources
If SEN needs are identified post-enrollment, schools work with families to provide reasonable accommodations or, if necessary, recommend more specialized placements
IEP (Individual Education Plan): Used for students with formally diagnosed SEN
ILP (Individual Learning Plan): Used for students requiring Tier 3 support without formal SEN diagnosis (e.g., Stage 1-2 EAL, severe learning gaps)
content.title: 8.5.3 Individual Education Plan (IEP) / Individual Learning Plan (ILP)

steps: Draft Plan (Learning Support Coordinator with input from teachers)

items: Review evaluation findings and current performance data
Identify priority areas for intervention
Set SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)

steps: IEP/ILP Meeting (within 2 weeks of case conference)

items: Attendees: Parents, Principal, Learning Support Coordinator, classroom/subject teachers, external specialists (optional)
Review and finalize: Present levels, goals, accommodations, services
Obtain signatures: Parents and school representatives sign agreement

steps: Documentation in EduNav

items: Full IEP/ILP uploaded to student profile
Accommodations flagged for all teachers to see

steps: Implementation and Monitoring

items: Review progress toward goals every 6-8 weeks (informal check-ins)
Formal IEP/ILP review every 6 months or annually
Background information, languages spoken, prior schooling
Summary of evaluation findings (cognitive, academic, behavioral)
Strengths and interests
Current academic achievement (EASE scores, Cambridge grades, teacher assessments)
Functional skills (communication, social-emotional, motor, adaptive behavior)
Impact of disability/learning gap on access to curriculum
Example: "By June 2026, [Student] will read grade-appropriate text at 120 words per minute with 95% accuracy, as measured by oral reading fluency probes administered monthly."
Example: "By June 2026, [Student] will independently solve multi-step word problems involving fractions with 80% accuracy on curriculum-based assessments."
Accommodations: Change how student accesses curriculum (e.g., extended time, assistive technology, preferential seating) without changing content/standards
Modifications: Change what student is expected to learn (e.g., reduced content, alternative assessments, modified grading)

content.text: See Section 8.5.4 for detailed examples F. Progress Monitoring Plan:

items: How goals will be measured (e.g., reading probes, math assessments, behavior tracking)
Frequency of data collection (weekly, monthly, quarterly)
Date of next IEP/ILP review meeting (typically 6-12 months)

content.text: Accommodations (Do NOT change curriculum expectations): Presentation Accommodations:

items: Preferential seating (front of class, away from distractions)
Enlarged text or printed materials (for visual impairments)
Audio recordings of texts or lectures
Bilingual glossaries and dictionaries (EAL)
Reduced visual clutter on worksheets
Use of assistive technology (text-to-speech software)
content.title: 8.5.4 Accommodations and Modifications Framework

items: Extended time on assessments (e.g., time-and-a-half, double time)
Oral responses instead of written (or vice versa)
Use of word processor/laptop for writing
Speech-to-text software
Use of calculator or manipulatives
Scribe for students with dysgraphia or physical disabilities
Small group or individual testing environment
Separate location for reduced distractions
Flexible seating options
Frequent breaks during assessments
Extended deadlines for assignments
Tests administered over multiple sessions

content.text: Modifications (DO change curriculum expectations): Content Modifications:

items: Reduced reading length or complexity
Simplified vocabulary and sentence structure
Alternative texts at lower reading level
Fewer learning objectives per lesson
Shorter assignments (e.g., answer 5 questions instead of 10)
Alternative assessment formats (e.g., poster instead of essay)
Modified grading criteria (effort-based, progress-based, or pass/fail)
Substituting Cambridge IGCSE First Language English with ESL
Enrolling in fewer IGCSE subjects
Following a modified curriculum pathway (e.g., skills-based vs. content-based)

content.text: Note: Modifications must be clearly documented in IEP/ILP and communicated to Cambridge (see Section 8.6 for access arrangements).

items: Student withdrawn from mainstream class for 45-60 minute daily sessions with Learning Support Teacher
Focus on foundational skills (phonics, number sense, etc.)
Risk: May miss core content; requires careful scheduling and coordination
content.title: 8.5.5 Tier 3 Instructional Models

items: Learning Support Teacher or Teaching Assistant works 1:1 with student in mainstream classroom
Provides pre-teaching, reteaching, or alternative instruction during lesson
Benefit: Student remains with peers; less stigmatizing
Learning Support Teacher co-teaches with subject teacher
Both teachers share planning and instruction
Best for: Multiple students with IEPs in same class
TA assigned to support student in mainstream classes
TA trained by Learning Support Coordinator on student's IEP accommodations
Note: TA does not replace teacher instruction; provides scaffolding only
Speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, counseling provided by external professionals
May occur at school (if provider comes on-site) or at external clinic
Documented in IEP; progress reports shared with school quarterly
IEP goal data: Collected weekly or monthly (depending on goal)
Academic benchmarks: EASE, Cambridge Checkpoint, teacher assessments
Behavioral data: If applicable (e.g., behavior tracking chart)
content.title: 8.5.6 Tier 3 Progress Monitoring and Annual Review

items: Timing: Every 12 months (or sooner if requested by parent/school)
Purpose:
Review progress toward IEP goals
Update present levels based on current data
Revise goals, accommodations, and services as needed
Determine continued eligibility for Tier 3 support
Attendees: Same as initial IEP meeting
Continue Tier 3: Student still requires intensive support; renew IEP
Transition to Tier 2: Student made significant progress; fade support to Tier 2 with monitoring
Exit to Tier 1: Student no longer requires specialized services (rare but possible with early intervention success)
Increase support: Student needs additional services or more intensive intervention

content.text: Cambridge Assessment International Education allows access arrangements for students with disabilities or learning differences to ensure fair access to examinations. Access arrangements are:

items: NOT a modification of the exam content or standards
Only available to students with documented needs and a history of using accommodations in class
content.title: 8.6.1 Overview of Access Arrangements

content.text: Key Principle: Access arrangements must reflect the student's normal way of working—the accommodations they routinely use in classroom assessments. Cross-reference: See Section 3.6 for Cambridge exam regulations and malpractice policies.

items: For students with processing speed difficulties (dyslexia, ADHD, etc.)
Most common access arrangement
Student receives 15 extra minutes per hour of exam time
content.title: 8.6.2 Types of Access Arrangements

items: For students with medical conditions, ADHD, or fatigue
Student may take short breaks during exam under supervision (time does not stop)
For students with dysgraphia or severe handwriting difficulties
Spell-check and grammar-check must be disabled
Used in separate room to avoid disturbing others
For students with severe reading difficulties (dyslexia) who cannot access written text independently
Note: Not allowed for language exams (English, foreign languages) unless specifically approved by Cambridge
For students with physical disabilities or severe dysgraphia who cannot write independently
Scribe writes exactly what student says; does not correct grammar or spelling
For students with ADHD or processing difficulties who need reminders to stay on task
Prompter sits near student and provides non-verbal cues to continue working

content.text: G. Modified Papers (Enlarged Text, Braille, Audio):

items: For students with visual or hearing impairments
School orders modified exam papers from Cambridge in advance
For students who require distraction-free environment or use assistive technology
Often combined with other arrangements (e.g., extra time \+ separate room)

content.text: 1. Determine Eligibility (conducted by Learning Support Coordinator or Exams Officer):

items: Student must have documented SEN (e.g., IEP, psychoeducational evaluation)
Accommodation must be student's normal way of working in school assessments (documented over time, at least 6+ months)
Accommodation must be necessary to give student fair access to exam
content.title: 8.6.3 Application Process for Access Arrangements

items: Schools apply via Cambridge Direct portal
Application requires:
Evidence of student's disability/learning difference (upload psychoeducational report, medical documentation)
Evidence of normal way of working (upload classroom assessment samples showing use of accommodation)
Detailed description of arrangement requested
At least 3 months before exam session (earlier is better to allow for processing)
Cambridge reviews and approves/denies application
If approved: School implements arrangement during exam
If denied: School can appeal with additional evidence or implement alternative support (e.g., if extra time denied, try rest breaks)
Exams Officer ensures access arrangements are in place (e.g., separate room booked, scribe trained, laptop configured)
Student receives accommodations exactly as approved by Cambridge
Document any issues and report to Cambridge if necessary

content.text: Planning Timeline: Documentation Required in Student File (EduNav):

items: Copy of Cambridge access arrangements approval letter
Evidence of normal way of working (classroom assessments)
IEP specifying accommodations
content.title: 8.6.4 Internal School Process

items: Cannot modify exam content: Student takes same exam as peers; no simplified language or reduced content
Cannot compensate for lack of knowledge: Access arrangements help student access the exam, not pass the exam
Cannot replace instruction: Student must receive quality teaching and intervention (Tiers 1-3)
content.title: 8.6.5 Important Limitations and Considerations

items: Insufficient evidence of disability
Accommodation not used regularly in classroom assessments
Requested arrangement would give "unfair advantage" (e.g., grammar-check enabled on word processor)
Schools may appeal denied applications with additional evidence
Allow 4+ weeks for appeal processing

content.text: While MTSS typically focuses on struggling learners, Eduversal also recognizes the need to support high-ability students who require enrichment and acceleration beyond grade-level expectations. Identification Criteria (Multiple Measures Required): Quantitative Measures: EASE scores consistently at 90th percentile or above for grade level

items: Cambridge Checkpoint scores significantly above age-expected levels
Advanced performance on Olimpiade Sains Indonesia (OSN) or EduOS assessments
Cognitive ability testing (if available): IQ ≥130 or 98th percentile Qualitative Measures:
Teacher nomination based on classroom observation
Portfolio evidence of exceptional work
Demonstrated creativity, critical thinking, or problem-solving beyond grade level
Parent input on interests and abilities Domain-Specific Giftedness:
Students may be identified in specific areas (math, science, language arts, arts, leadership)
Does not require across-the-board high achievement
content.title: 8.7.1 Identification of Gifted and Talented Students

items: Quarterly SST meetings review data for potential G\&T identification
Learning Support Coordinator maintains register of identified G\&T students in EduNav
Parents notified of identification and provided with enrichment opportunities
Differentiation by challenge: Extending depth and complexity for high achievers
Open-ended tasks that allow for multiple entry points and sophisticated responses
Inquiry-based learning and student-led investigations
Flexible grouping: High-ability students work together on advanced tasks
content.title: 8.7.2 G\&T Support Models

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 5.7.2 for differentiation strategies including challenge/extension. Tier 2 (Targeted Enrichment): A. Subject Acceleration:

items: Student moves ahead in one subject area while remaining with age-peers in others
Example: Grade 5 student takes Grade 7 Math
Requires: Parent approval, schedule coordination, ongoing monitoring
Pre-assess student on upcoming unit
If student demonstrates mastery, excuses from portions of instruction
Student engages in enrichment activities while peers learn content
Example: Student masters multiplication; works on logic puzzles during multiplication unit
High-ability students withdrawn 1-2 periods per week for advanced content
Topics beyond standard curriculum (e.g., robotics, creative writing, philosophy, advanced science experiments)
Facilitated by G\&T coordinator, specialist teacher, or external instructor
EduOS (Eduversal Olimpiade Sains): School-based science olympiad preparation program
OSN/KSN: National science olympiad (Olimpiade Sains Nasional, now Kompetisi Sains Nasional)
Math competitions: AMC, MATHCOUNTS, local/international competitions
Cambridge competitions: Cambridge Outstanding Learner Awards
After-school or lunchtime coaching sessions

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 7.6 for EduOS and academic competition programs. Tier 3 (Highly Gifted/Intensive Support): A. Grade Acceleration (Whole-Grade Skip):

items: Student advances one full grade level
Rare: Requires comprehensive evaluation (academic, social-emotional, physical maturity)
Decision made by Principal in consultation with parents, teachers, and psychologist
Typically only considered for exceptionally gifted students (99th percentile across all areas)

content.text: B. Individual Learning Plan (ILP) for G\&T:

items: For students whose needs cannot be met through standard acceleration/enrichment
May include:
Independent study projects with mentor teacher
Dual enrollment (if upper secondary): Taking university courses online or in-person
Mentorship with external expert in field of interest
Online advanced courses (e.g., Stanford EPGY, CTY Online)
Students take IGCSE exams 1-2 years early in strength area(s)
Example: Take IGCSE Additional Math in Year 9 instead of Year 11
Requires Cambridge registration; no age restrictions for IGCSE entry
Perfectionism: Fear of failure; reluctance to take risks
Asynchronous development: Advanced cognitively but age-appropriate socially/emotionally
Underachievement: May not be challenged and develop poor work habits
Social isolation: May struggle to find intellectual peers
Existential questioning: Heightened awareness of complex world issues
content.title: 8.7.3 Social-Emotional Needs of Gifted Learners

items: Counseling support focused on perfectionism, stress management, identity development
Peer groups: Connect G\&T students for collaboration and friendship
Growth mindset messaging: Emphasize effort, challenge-seeking, and learning from failure
Differentiation that includes appropriate challenge (avoid "busywork enrichment")

content.text: Definition: Students who are both gifted AND have a learning disability (e.g., gifted with dyslexia, ADHD, or autism). Identification Challenge:

items: Strengths may mask weaknesses (and vice versa)
May appear "average" because giftedness compensates for disability
Requires careful observation and comprehensive assessment
content.title: 8.7.4 Twice-Exceptional (2E) Students

items: Provide both G\&T enrichment AND SEN accommodations/interventions
IEP addresses learning disability supports; G\&T plan addresses enrichment
Example: Student with dyslexia receives Tier 2 reading intervention but also takes advanced math

subsections.title: LEARNING SUPPORT TEAM STRUCTURE AND ROLES

content.text: Core Team Members: A. Learning Support Coordinator (LSC)

items: Qualifications: Teaching certification \+ SEN/Inclusion certification (e.g., SENCO, special education degree)
Responsibilities:
Coordinate MTSS/RTI implementation across school
Lead Student Support Team (SST) meetings
Conduct internal SEN assessments and develop IEPs/ILPs
Train teachers on differentiation, accommodations, and inclusive practices
Liaise with external specialists and manage referrals
Maintain SEN register and documentation in EduNav
Coordinate Cambridge access arrangements applications
Supervise Teaching Assistants assigned to SEN students
content.title: 8.8.1 Learning Support Team Composition

items: Qualifications: TESOL, TEFL, or EAL specialist certification
Responsibilities:
Administer EAL proficiency assessments for new students
Assign EAL stages and recommend support tiers
Provide direct EAL instruction (pull-out or push-in)
Train teachers on EAL instructional strategies
Monitor EAL student progress and coordinate exit decisions
Develop EAL curriculum and resources
Qualifications: Psychology or counseling degree; preferably licensed
Responsibilities:
Provide individual/group counseling for social-emotional needs
Conduct behavioral assessments and functional behavior analyses
Support Tier 2/3 behavioral interventions
Crisis intervention and mental health referrals
Collaborate with LSC on 2E students and students with ADHD/anxiety/depression
Work with LSC to identify students needing support at their year level
Attend SST meetings for students in their year group
Communicate with parents about support plans
Monitor pastoral and academic wellbeing
Implement Tier 1 differentiation and accommodations
Collect progress monitoring data for Tier 2/3 students
Attend SST meetings for students they teach
Provide input on IEP/ILP goals and accommodations
Support implementation of IEP accommodations in classrooms
Deliver Tier 2 interventions (if trained)
Supervised by LSC; receive regular training on student needs
Coordinates Cambridge access arrangements applications
Implements approved accommodations during exams
Maintains records of exam accommodations

content.text: Purpose: Collaborative problem-solving to support struggling students Frequency:

items: Scheduled: Every 6 weeks during term
Emergency: Called as needed for urgent concerns
content.title: 8.8.2 Student Support Team (SST) Meeting Structure

content.text: Attendees: LSC (chair), YLC, relevant teachers, EAL Coordinator (if applicable), Counselor (if applicable) Agenda Template:

steps: Review of previous action items (5 minutes)
New referrals (10-15 minutes per student)

items: Present concern and data
Determine tier of support needed
Assign action items

steps: Progress monitoring of existing Tier 2/3 students (5-10 minutes per student)

items: Review intervention data
Decide: continue, adjust, exit, or escalate

steps: G\&T nominations (as needed)
Whole-school trends (if time allows)

items: Are many students struggling with same skill? May indicate need for Tier 1 improvement
SST meeting minutes recorded in EduNav
Action items assigned with deadlines
Parents notified of decisions within 1 week

subsections.title: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) as Tier 1

content.text: Universal Design for Learning (UDL) serves as the foundational cornerstone of Eduversal's Multi-Tiered System of Supports, operating as the essential infrastructure of Tier 1 universal support. While Section 8.2 outlines Quality First Teaching expectations, this section provides the specific UDL framework that makes those expectations operational and effective for all learners. Positioning UDL in the MTSS Triangle: In our MTSS framework (illustrated in Section 8.1.2), UDL is not a separate intervention—it is the proactive design philosophy that strengthens the base of the pyramid:

items: Tier 1 (80-85% of students): UDL provides the flexible, accessible foundation that allows most students to succeed without additional support
Tier 2 (10-15% of students): Strong UDL reduces the number of students requiring targeted interventions by addressing barriers before they become problems
Tier 3 (5-10% of students): Even students with intensive needs benefit from UDL-designed environments, which complement their individualized supports
content.title: 8.9.1 UDL within MTSS/RTI Framework

content.text: The Prevention Role of UDL: Research shows that poor Tier 1 instruction is the primary cause of over-identification for Tier 2 and 3 interventions. When curriculum and instruction are designed with accessibility barriers built in, students who could succeed with better design are unnecessarily funneled into intervention systems. UDL prevents this by:

steps: Eliminating instructional barriers proactively: Rather than waiting for students to fail and then providing accommodations reactively, UDL builds flexibility into lessons from the start
Reducing false positives: Students struggling due to instructional design (not learning difficulties) are not misidentified as needing specialized support
Providing early data: When UDL is implemented well, teachers quickly identify students who genuinely need Tier 2/3 support because barriers have already been minimized

content.text: Connection to Existing Section 8 Components: ![][image8] Key Principle: UDL is universal—it benefits all students, not just those with identified needs. A lesson designed with UDL principles helps the gifted student, the EAL learner, the student with ADHD, and the neurotypical student sitting in the middle of the class. This is what makes it the ideal Tier 1 approach. Cross-reference: See Section 5.7 for curriculum-level differentiation strategies and Section 7 for UDL in lesson planning (Section 7.X when available).

Understanding the distinction between UDL, accommodations, and modifications is critical for effective implementation and ensures compliance with Cambridge and Indonesian regulations. Definitions and Examples A. Universal Design for Learning (UDL) Definition: Proactive instructional design that provides multiple pathways for all students to access content, engage with learning, and demonstrate knowledge—built into the lesson from the start. Key Characteristics:

items: Available to everyone in the class
No stigma—presented as standard teaching practice
Does not change curriculum content or standards
Does not require IEP/ILP or formal documentation
Aligns with "normal way of working" for Cambridge access arrangements
content.title: 8.9.2 UDL vs. Accommodations vs. Modifications

content.text: Examples in Eduversal Classrooms: ![][image9] B. Accommodations Definition: Changes in how a student accesses curriculum or demonstrates learning, without changing the content, standards, or expectations. Provided to individual students based on documented needs (typically via IEP/ILP). Key Characteristics:

items: Individualized for specific students
Documented in IEP/ILP (Section 8.5.3)
Does not lower academic expectations
May require Cambridge approval for exams (Section 8.6)
Must be student's "normal way of working" over time

content.text: Examples: ![][image10] C. Modifications Definition: Changes in what a student is expected to learn—altering content, reducing complexity, or changing performance standards. Represents a different curriculum or expectation than peers. Key Characteristics:

items: Changes learning outcomes for the student
Documented in IEP/ILP
May affect grading, credits, or Cambridge qualification earned
Used only when accommodations \+ UDL insufficient
Carefully considered due to impact on student trajectory

content.text: Examples: ![][image11] Decision Framework: UDL, Accommodation, or Modification? Example Decision Tree in Action: Scenario: Dimas (Grade 7) is struggling to complete written assignments in English class. Step 1: Is the barrier universal (could affect many students)?

items: Yes → Strong UDL could help: Provide sentence starters, allow use of word processor, offer audio recording option to whole class
Outcome: Dimas improves along with 10 other students; no IEP needed

content.text: Step 2: Did UDL help Dimas enough?

items: No → Dimas still significantly behind despite UDL supports
Action: Refer to SST; assess for dysgraphia; develop IEP
Accommodation: Extra time on written assessments; use of word processor (spell-check disabled); dictation option for long assignments
NOT Modification: Dimas still writes to same rubric as peers; content not reduced

content.text: Step 4: When does modification become necessary?

items: Only if: After 2 terms of accommodations \+ Tier 2 writing intervention, Dimas cannot produce grade-level work
Modification example: Reduce essay length from 500 words to 250 words; use modified rubric with lower expectations
Impact: Must be clearly documented; affects Cambridge exam eligibility

content.text: Why UDL Reduces Need for Accommodations When UDL is implemented well at Tier 1, fewer students require formal accommodations because:

steps: Barriers are pre-emptively removed: Captions, graphic organizers, flexible seating benefit students who might otherwise need accommodations
Accommodations become "normal way of working": If whole class uses word processors, the dyslexic student's use is not stigmatized—it's just how the class works
Evidence for Cambridge access arrangements: When UDL tools are standard practice, it's easy to document "normal way of working" for access arrangement applications (Section 8.6)

items: Without UDL: Teacher lectures for 40 minutes; student with ADHD cannot focus → Accommodation needed: preferential seating, fidget tool, extra time due to missed instruction
With UDL: Teacher chunks lecture into 10-minute segments with think-pair-share, movement breaks, and visual note-taking → Student with ADHD succeeds; accommodation may not be needed

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 8.5.4 for comprehensive accommodations and modifications matrix, and Section 8.6 for Cambridge access arrangements process.

UDL's strength lies in its ability to proactively address common barriers that students face in Eduversal classrooms. This section provides evidence-based UDL strategies for the most frequent challenges, demonstrating how Tier 1 design prevents the need for Tier 2/3 interventions. A. Reading Difficulties (Dyslexia, Decoding Challenges, Low Reading Fluency) Common Barriers:

items: Cannot decode text at grade level
Slow reading speed prevents completing assignments
Comprehension suffers due to energy spent decoding
Avoidance of reading tasks; learned helplessness
content.title: 8.9.3 UDL for Common Learning Barriers

content.text: UDL Strategies (Tier 1 for ALL Students): ![][image12]![][image13] Classroom Vignette: Teacher: Ms. Putri (Grade 8 English) Lesson: Reading comprehension of an IGCSE Literature text (excerpt from Animal Farm) UDL Implementation:

steps: Before reading: Ms. Putri pre-teaches 8 key vocabulary words (allegory, propaganda, tyranny) using visuals and Indonesian translations
During reading: Students can choose to: (a) read silently, (b) listen to audiobook with text, (c) partner-read
Text support: Ms. Putri provides annotated version with important passages highlighted and margin notes
After reading: Students choose to demonstrate comprehension via: written paragraph, concept map, or recorded voice note explaining symbolism

items: Fajar (dyslexia, Tier 2 reading support) uses audiobook and writes shorter paragraph → succeeds in Tier 1 with UDL
Rina (EAL Stage 3) benefits from vocabulary pre-teaching and chooses voice recording → no additional support needed
12 other students without identified needs also use audiobook, annotations, or alternative responses → ALL students access grade-level literature

content.text: B. Attention and Executive Function Challenges Common Barriers:

items: Cannot sustain attention during long lectures or independent work
Loses track of multi-step tasks; forgets materials or deadlines
Difficulty inhibiting impulses (calling out, moving around)
Struggles with time management and planning

content.text: UDL Strategies (Tier 1 for ALL Students): ⇒Table Reqiured \<= Classroom Vignette: Teacher: Pak Joko (Grade 5 Science) Lesson: Investigating the water cycle (60-minute practical lesson) UDL Implementation:

steps: Visual schedule: Pak Joko displays 5-part lesson structure on board: (1) Review concepts (10 min), (2) Demo experiment (10 min), (3) Group investigation (20 min), (4) Record findings (10 min), (5) Share conclusions (10 min)
Chunking with movement: After each 10-minute segment, students do 1-minute stretch or partner share
Flexible seating: During group work, students can stand at lab tables, sit on floor with clipboards, or use traditional desks
Checklists: Each group has a laminated task checklist: ☐ Set up equipment, ☐ Heat water, ☐ Observe condensation, ☐ Record data, ☐ Answer questions
Visual timer: Pak Joko projects 20-minute countdown during investigation phase

items: Doni (ADHD, no IEP yet) uses standing desk, follows checklist, completes investigation → succeeds with UDL; Tier 2 referral unnecessary
Entire class stays on task longer; behavior incidents decrease
Pak Joko spends less time redirecting students, more time facilitating learning

content.text: C. Language Barriers (EAL Learners \- Stages 2-4) Common Barriers:

items: Cannot understand academic vocabulary or complex sentence structures
Lacks background knowledge assumed by curriculum (cultural references, prior learning)
Struggles to express ideas in English even when understands concept
Slow processing time for English input

content.text: UDL Strategies (Tier 1 for ALL Students): (Note: These UDL strategies complement EAL-specific interventions described in Section 8.4. UDL is for everyone; EAL support is for specific students.) ⇒Table Reqiured \<= Classroom Vignette: Teacher: Ms. Sari (Grade 7 Humanities \- Geography) Lesson: Climate zones and their characteristics UDL Implementation:

steps: Visual \+ text: Ms. Sari shows world climate map on screen; explains tropical, temperate, polar zones using images of each (rainforest, beach, snow)
Bilingual glossary: Students receive handout with terms: Climate zone (zona iklim), Precipitation (curah hujan), Latitude (garis lintang)
Think-Pair-Share: "Why do you think the equator has more rain than the poles?" → Students think silently (1 min), discuss with partner (2 min), share
Sentence frames: "I think \\ because \." "This is similar to \\ because \." posted on board
Multimodal assessment: Students create poster showing one climate zone with: (a) drawn/printed image, (b) 5 key characteristics labeled, (c) example country

items: Amir (EAL Stage 3) uses bilingual glossary, discusses in Indonesian with partner during pair work, creates poster with drawings and short phrases → succeeds without pull-out EAL support
Lina (EAL Stage 4) challenges herself to use sentence frames for oral explanation
Entire class benefits from visual supports and multimodal assessment; achievement gap between EAL and native speakers narrows

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 8.4 for full EAL framework including placement, proficiency stages, and exit criteria. This section focuses on UDL strategies that support language learners within Tier 1 instruction. D. Sensory and Physical Needs Common Barriers:

items: Overwhelmed or under-stimulated by sensory environment (noise, light, textures)
Difficulty with fine motor tasks (handwriting, cutting, manipulating small objects)
Cannot see/hear instruction clearly

content.text: UDL Strategies (Tier 1 for ALL Students): ⇒Table Reqiured \<= Classroom Vignette: Teacher: Bu Rina (Grade 4 Math) Lesson: Fractions (adding fractions with like denominators) UDL Implementation:

steps: Manipulatives: Bu Rina provides fraction strips, fraction circles, and digital fraction models (on tablets) for whole class
Flexible seating: Students can work at tables, on floor mats, or standing at whiteboard easels
Choice in practice: Students choose: (a) worksheet with pencil, (b) practice problems on tablet, (c) build fractions with manipulatives and photograph answers
Noise options: Students who need quiet can wear headphones or work in designated "quiet zone"; students who prefer collaborative work stay at tables

items: Andi (autism, sensory sensitivities) uses headphones and fraction circles at quiet zone desk → masters fractions without modification; no IEP needed
Budi (dysgraphia) types answers on tablet instead of handwriting
Entire class engages with fractions multimodally; conceptual understanding improves school-wide

content.text: A UDL-designed physical and visual environment removes barriers before students even enter the room. This section provides practical guidance for creating inclusive classroom spaces that support all learners. A. Physical Space Considerations Flexible Seating Options Rationale: Students have diverse needs for movement, posture, and personal space. Traditional rows of desks accommodate only a narrow range of learners. Implementation at Eduversal: ![][image14] Example Floor Plan \- Grade 7 Classroom: (Diagram would show: traditional desk rows for direct instruction, 2 standing desks in back corner, cushion reading nook near bookshelf, collaborative table groups near whiteboard, 2 wobble stools at individual study carrels) Seating Norms:

items: Students choose seating appropriate for task (teacher provides guidance)
During direct instruction: students must be able to see board (standing desks in back OK; floor cushions not OK)
During independent work: students can move to preferred seat
During assessments: students typically use traditional desks (unless IEP specifies otherwise)
content.title: 8.9.4 Classroom Environment Design

items: Designate areas where students can:
Stand while working (at tall tables or counter space)
Pace (for students who think while moving)
Take brain breaks (stretch zone near door)
Traffic flow: Arrange furniture so students can move without disrupting others
Quiet Zone: 2-3 desks in corner with visual barrier (bookshelf, divider); students use when need to reduce stimulation
Rules: Max 2 students at a time; must be working (not socializing); bring noise-canceling headphones
Sensory Tools Available:
Basket of fidgets (stress balls, putty, tangle toys) \- students can borrow during lessons
Weighted lap pads (for students who need pressure for focus)
Timer for movement breaks (e.g., after 20 min seated work, students can stretch for 1 min)
Wheelchair access: Ensure aisles wide enough (minimum 90 cm); adjustable desks if needed
Visual impairment: Student seated near board; materials available in enlarged print
Hearing impairment: Student seated front/center; teacher uses microphone if available

content.text: B. Visual Supports and Environmental Print Purpose: Reduce cognitive load by providing visual cues, memory aids, and language scaffolds accessible to all students at all times. Word Walls (All Classrooms K-10)

items: What: Display of key vocabulary with definitions, images, and example sentences
Organization: By unit/topic; color-coded by part of speech or category
Use: Students refer to word wall during discussions, writing, assessments
Example (Grade 8 Science \- Cells unit):
Word: Cell membrane (blue card)
Image: Diagram of cell with membrane highlighted
Definition: Outer layer that controls what enters and exits the cell
Indonesian: Membran sel
Sentence: "The cell membrane protects the cell like a gate."
Daily agenda: Posted on board or slide at start of lesson; checked off as completed
Example: "1. Warm-up (5 min) ✓ 2. New vocab (10 min) ✓ 3. Group activity (20 min) → 4. Exit ticket (5 min)"
Weekly overview: Calendar showing major assignments, tests, due dates
Benefits: Students with executive function challenges know what's coming; reduces anxiety for students with autism; ALL students manage time
What: Step-by-step procedures, rubrics, strategies that students use repeatedly
Examples:
"How to Solve Word Problems" (Math)
"Peer Editing Checklist" (English)
"Scientific Method Steps" (Science)
Design: Large font, visuals/icons, laminated for durability
Location: Posted at eye level; easily visible from student work areas
WALT (We Are Learning To) and WILF (What I'm Looking For): Posted at start of lesson
WALT: "We are learning to add fractions with like denominators"
WILF: "I will be able to solve 8 out of 10 problems correctly"
Rubrics: Displayed during task so students self-assess
Benefits: Students with processing challenges know expectations; ALL students self-monitor progress
Classroom rules poster: Positive language ("We listen when others speak" vs. "No talking"); icons for non-readers
Routines flowcharts: "What to do when you finish early," "How to ask for help"
Benefits: Students with autism/ADHD refer to visuals for routines; EAL learners see expectations; ALL students internalize norms

content.text: C. Technology Infrastructure for UDL Device Access (1:1 or Shared)

items: Ideal: Every student has device (laptop, tablet, Chromebook) for assistive technology use
Minimum: Shared devices available (e.g., 10 tablets for class of 25); students request as needed
BYOD (Bring Your Own Device): If financially feasible for families, students bring personal devices; school provides loaners for students without access

content.text: Assistive Technology Available to ALL Students ![][image15] Teacher Technology Setup

items: Projector/interactive whiteboard: Display visual schedules, videos, digital notes
Document camera: Show student work, demonstrate hands-on tasks visually
Microphone system (if budget allows): Amplify teacher voice for students with hearing difficulties; benefits ALL students in large rooms
Classroom website/LMS page: Post assignments, resources, video tutorials so students access 24/7

content.text: D. UDL-Ready Classroom Checklist For Principals/Learning Support Coordinators conducting classroom walkthroughs: Scoring:

items: 15-18 checked: Exemplary UDL classroom environment
10-14 checked: Developing; identify 2-3 priorities for improvement
\<10 checked: Needs support; provide professional development and resources

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 7 for lesson observation protocols; UDL environment can be integrated into walkthrough rubrics.

Effective Tier 1 UDL is not intuitive—it requires systematic data collection to ensure that universal supports are actually universal and that the right students are identified for Tier 2/3. This section outlines how to use data to monitor UDL effectiveness and refine MTSS implementation. A. Metrics for Tier 1 UDL Effectiveness Key Question: "Are 80-85% of our students succeeding with Tier 1 alone, or are we over-relying on Tier 2/3 interventions?" Data to Collect: ![][image17] Example Data Dashboard (displayed in Principal's office or Learning Support Team meetings): ![][image18] B. Identifying When UDL Tier 1 Isn't Enough Scenario 1: UDL is strong, but student still struggles → Legitimate Tier 2/3 need Data Pattern:

items: Teacher implemented UDL checklist with fidelity (score 4.5/5 on walkthrough)
Classroom observation shows: visual supports, flexible seating, choice in assessments, chunked instruction
But: Rina (Grade 6) still cannot decode grade-level text after 8 weeks
content.title: 8.9.5 Data-Driven UDL Implementation

content.text: Conclusion: UDL alone insufficient; Rina likely has dyslexia → Refer to SST for Tier 2 literacy intervention \+ potential psychoeducational evaluation Example SST Documentation: "Rina has been in Ms. Putri's UDL-rich classroom since August (documented: text-to-speech available, graphic organizers provided, vocabulary pre-taught). Despite these supports \+ 8 weeks of small-group reading practice, Rina's DIBELS score remains at 45 wpm (grade expectation: 110 wpm). Recommendation: Tier 2 Orton-Gillingham reading intervention 4x/week \+ refer for dyslexia assessment." Scenario 2: UDL is weak, student struggles → Strengthen Tier 1 first Data Pattern:

items: Teacher UDL checklist score: 1.8/5 (no visual supports, lecture-heavy, no choice)
Multiple students in class struggling (6 out of 22 referred to SST in one quarter)

content.text: Conclusion: Problem is instruction, not students → Do NOT refer all 6 to Tier 2; provide teacher coaching on UDL first Example Principal Action: "After reviewing Mr. Budi's classroom (Grade 4), I observed minimal UDL implementation. Rather than placing 6 students in Tier 2 interventions (overtaxing our resources), I will: (1) Provide Mr. Budi with UDL coaching cycles (2x in next 6 weeks), (2) Model UDL lesson, (3) Re-assess student progress after Tier 1 improved. Goal: Reduce Tier 2 referrals by strengthening Tier 1." C. Data-Informed UDL Improvement Cycle Quarterly Review Process (led by Principal \+ Learning Support Coordinator): Step 1: Collect Data (Week 1 of each quarter)

items: MTSS tier percentages
EASE benchmark results (if assessment window)
Tier 2 intervention effectiveness data
Teacher UDL checklist scores (from walkthroughs)
Which classrooms/grade levels have high Tier 2 referral rates?
Are specific student subgroups overrepresented in Tier 2 (EAL? Boys? Specific subjects?)
Which teachers have strong UDL implementation and low Tier 2 referrals?

content.text: Step 3: Develop Action Plan (Week 3) Example Action Plan (Q3, January 2026): ![][image19] Step 4: Implement and Monitor (Weeks 4-12)

items: Carry out actions
Collect ongoing data (e.g., Mr. Agus's walkthrough scores improve from 1.8 to 3.5)
Did Grade 7 Math Tier 2 rate decrease?
Did EAL students' success in Tier 1 improve?
Adjust plan for next quarter

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 1.7 (Supervisi quality assurance) for how Eduversal's central team reviews school MTSS data during visits.

Strong UDL implementation directly supports Eduversal schools' ability to secure Cambridge access arrangements for students with SEN—and reduces the need for such arrangements by making accommodations "normal way of working." A. How UDL Connects to Access Arrangements Recap (from Section 8.6): Cambridge allows access arrangements (extra time, word processor, scribe, etc.) for students with documented needs, IF the arrangement is the student's normal way of working in classroom assessments. UDL Makes This Easy:

items: Scenario WITHOUT UDL:
Teacher uses pencil-and-paper tests only
Rafi (dyslexia) requests use of laptop for IGCSE exams
Problem: Cambridge requires proof Rafi regularly uses laptop in class → Evidence is weak because only Rafi has laptop, not normal classroom practice
Scenario WITH UDL:
Teacher provides ALL students option to type or handwrite assessments
Rafi consistently chooses laptop
Benefit: Clear evidence of "normal way of working" → Cambridge approval straightforward
content.title: 8.9.6 UDL in Access Arrangements (Cambridge Exams)

content.text: Key Principle: When UDL tools are available to everyone, students with SEN use them routinely without stigma—building the evidence trail needed for access arrangements. B. UDL Tools as "Normal Way of Working" Evidence Example Cambridge Application (strengthened by UDL): Student: Rina (Grade 10, dyslexia \+ ADHD) Access Arrangements Requested: Extra time (25%) \+ word processor \+ separate room Evidence Submitted:

steps: Psychoeducational report: Diagnosis of dyslexia and ADHD (2024)
IEP: Documents accommodations (extra time, word processor, separate room)
Normal Way of Working Documentation:

items: 6 months of classroom assessment work samples showing:
All essays typed on laptop (UDL practice: whole class can type)
Extra time used on 4 out of 5 unit tests (UDL practice: teacher allows flexible pacing)
Rina regularly uses quiet zone for assessments (UDL practice: quiet zone available to all)
Teacher statement: "Rina has used laptop and extra time in my English class since Grade 9. This is standard practice in our classroom—many students type, and flexible pacing is available."

content.text: Cambridge Decision: Approved (strong evidence; accommodations clearly aligned with classroom practice) C. When UDL Alone Is NOT Sufficient for Access Arrangements Some access arrangements (e.g., scribe, reader, modified papers) are NOT UDL because they are individualized, one-on-one supports. These require:

items: Formal IEP/ILP documentation
Evidence of significant disability
May require more extensive justification to Cambridge

content.text: Examples: ![][image21] Important: Even if UDL cannot provide these supports, UDL still benefits these students by reducing other barriers (visuals for student with reading disability, chunked instructions for student with ADHD). Cross-reference: See Section 8.6 for full Cambridge access arrangements application process and timeline.

Sustainable UDL requires a systems-level approach—it cannot rely on individual teacher initiative. This section outlines leadership actions, monitoring strategies, and cultural shifts necessary for schoolwide UDL. A. Leadership Role: Principal and Learning Support Coordinator Principal's Actions:

steps: Champion UDL Publicly
content.title: 8.9.7 Whole-School UDL Implementation

items: Include UDL in school vision/mission talks with staff, parents, students

Example: "At EDU Prestige, we design learning so every student can succeed. You'll notice our classrooms have flexible seating, visual supports, and technology accessible to all. This is UDL—our Tier 1 promise."

steps: Allocate Resources

items: Budget for: flexible seating, assistive technology, bilingual glossaries, manipulatives
Time: Protect PLC time for teachers to collaborate on UDL lesson design

steps: Model UDL in Leadership

items: Staff meetings use visual agendas, provide handouts in advance, offer choice in PD activities
"Walk the talk": If we expect UDL in classrooms, model it in adult learning

steps: Monitor Implementation

items: Include UDL elements in classroom walkthroughs (Section 8.X.4.D checklist)
Review MTSS data quarterly (Section 8.X.5); celebrate schools/teachers with strong Tier 1 outcomes

steps: Hold Teachers Accountable (Supportively)

items: UDL expectations in job descriptions and appraisal rubrics
Coaching, not punishment, for teachers needing UDL support
Recognize exemplary UDL practice publicly (e.g., "Teacher of the Month" highlight)

steps: Provide UDL Professional Development

items: Lead workshops on UDL principles (Section 8.X.8)
Model UDL strategies in co-taught lessons
Create resource bank (templates, videos, exemplar lessons)

steps: Support Individual Teachers

items: Co-plan UDL lessons with teachers needing support
Conduct UDL classroom walkthroughs with feedback
Troubleshoot barriers: "Your classroom has no visual supports—let's create 3 anchor charts together this week."

steps: Coordinate UDL and MTSS Data

items: Track which teachers have high Tier 2 referral rates → Provide UDL coaching
Document UDL practices for Cambridge access arrangements (Section 8.X.6)

steps: Liaise with EAL Coordinator

items: Ensure Tier 1 UDL strategies support EAL learners (Section 8.X.3.C)
Co-present PD on "UDL for Linguistically Diverse Classrooms"

content.text: B. Teacher Training: Building UDL Capacity School-Wide Year 1: Foundation August PD Week (3-hour workshop):

items: Content:
What is UDL? Three principles (representation, engagement, expression)
UDL vs. differentiation vs. accommodations (Section 8.X.2)
UDL classroom environment checklist (Section 8.X.4.D)
Activity: Teachers audit their classrooms; identify 3 UDL improvements to make before students arrive
Deliverable: Each teacher creates one UDL-enhanced lesson plan
September PLC: Share UDL classroom setups; troubleshoot challenges ("I don't have space for flexible seating—ideas?")
October PLC: Focus on UDL for reading barriers (Section 8.X.3.A); watch video of exemplar lesson
November PLC: Focus on UDL for attention/executive function (Section 8.X.3.B)
January PLC: Focus on UDL for EAL learners (Section 8.X.3.C)
March PLC: Review MTSS data; celebrate success stories ("In Mr. Joko's class, Tier 2 referrals dropped from 8 to 3 students after implementing UDL!")
Coaching Cycles: LSC or HOD conducts 3-week coaching cycles with teachers:
Week 1: Pre-observation meeting (plan UDL lesson together)
Week 2: Observe lesson; collect data on UDL implementation
Week 3: Post-observation feedback; refine next lesson
Peer Observations: Teachers observe colleagues skilled in UDL; debrief in PLC
Lesson Study: Teachers collaboratively design, teach, observe, and refine one UDL lesson as team
UDL embedded in school culture; new teachers inducted immediately
Advanced topics: UDL for assessment design, UDL in online/blended learning
Teachers present UDL innovations at Eduversal network meetings or conferences

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 7.5 for Professional Learning Community (PLC) structures and Section 12 for professional development planning. C. Monitoring: Classroom Walkthroughs with UDL Lens Integration with Existing DTP Observations: Eduversal's Development of Teaching Proficiency (DTP) program includes regular lesson observations (Section 7.3). Principals and Academic Coordinators can integrate UDL monitoring into these observations. UDL Look-Fors During Walkthrough: (5-10 minute informal observation) Feedback Examples:

items: Strength: "I noticed you provided sentence frames for the discussion—great UDL for EAL learners and all students practicing academic language!"

Growth Area: "Consider chunking your direct instruction into 10-min segments with partner talk in between—this UDL strategy would help students with attention challenges and increase engagement for everyone."
Resource Suggestion: "I see you lecture for 30 min straight. Let's co-plan a lesson using the UDL chunking strategy (Section 8.X.3.B)—I'll model one next week if helpful."
Informal walkthroughs: Weekly (Principal/AP walk-throughs); focus on UDL environment
Formal observations: 2-3x/year per teacher (DTP cycle); UDL integrated into observation rubric

content.text: Cross-reference: See Section 7.3.4 for lesson observation rubric; UDL can be added as a specific criterion. D. Parent Communication: Explaining UDL Approach Parents may notice changes in classrooms (flexible seating, technology use, choice) and wonder why their child's classroom looks different from their own school experience. Proactive communication prevents concerns. Parent Orientation (August):

items: In-person session (optional but encouraged) or video:
"What is UDL? How it helps your child"
Show classroom photos: flexible seating, visual supports, technology
Explain: "We design lessons so every child can learn in their own way."
Q\&A: Address common concerns
"Will my child be 'held back' by working in a mixed-ability classroom?" → No, UDL provides challenge and support simultaneously
"Is UDL only for students with special needs?" → No, UDL benefits ALL learners; high achievers get choice and challenge too
Newsletter articles: "This month, our teachers are focusing on UDL strategies for reading" (brief explanation \+ photo of word wall)
Parent-teacher conferences: Teachers explain how UDL supports student's progress
Example: "Your daughter Rina uses text-to-speech for Science readings—this is available to the whole class and helps her access grade-level content while we work on her decoding skills."
Report card comments: Teachers can reference UDL
Example: "Budi thrives when given choice in demonstrating learning—his video project on ecosystems showed deep understanding of the food chain."
UDL does NOT mean "dumbing down"
High achievers benefit from choice (can select more challenging options)
Extension activities built into UDL design
Gifted students often have learning differences (2E)—UDL supports them too

content.text: Sample Parent Letter (sent home in August): Dear EDU Prestige Families, This year, you may notice some changes in our classrooms—flexible seating options, technology tools available to all students, and varied ways for students to show what they know. These are part of our commitment to Universal Design for Learning (UDL). What is UDL? UDL is an instructional approach that removes barriers to learning by providing multiple pathways for every student to succeed. Think of it like building a ramp in addition to stairs—the ramp helps some people, but it doesn't hurt anyone, and many people benefit (parents with strollers, delivery carts, etc.). UDL means:

items: Lessons that use visuals, videos, and hands-on activities (not just lectures)
Students choosing how to demonstrate learning (written, oral, visual, digital)
Classrooms with flexible seating, visual supports, and technology accessible to all

content.text: Who benefits? Everyone! UDL helps English language learners, students with learning differences, gifted students, and all learners in between. Questions? Please reach out to your child's teacher or our Learning Support Coordinator (lsc@edupresti ge.sch.id). We're excited about this approach and look forward to sharing your child's progress! Warm regards, Principal [Name]

This section provides a concrete, replicable PD sequence for introducing UDL to Eduversal teaching staff. Phase 1: Foundation (August PD Week \- Year 1) Session 1: What is UDL? (90 minutes) Objectives:

items: Define UDL and its three principles
Distinguish UDL from differentiation, accommodations, modifications
Understand UDL's role in MTSS Tier 1
content.title: 8.9.8 Professional Development: UDL Training Sequence

steps: Hook (10 min): Show video of UDL classroom vs. traditional classroom; discuss differences
Input (30 min): Present UDL principles with examples from Eduversal context (this section's examples)
Activity (30 min): Small groups analyze lesson plans; identify which elements are UDL vs. accommodations
Discussion (15 min): How would UDL address the most common student struggles in your classroom?
Closing (5 min): Teachers commit to one UDL change to implement Week 1

Anticipated objections and barriers to UDL implementation—and practical responses for principals and LSCs. Challenge 1: "UDL takes too much time—I already have too much to plan!" Root Concern: Teachers overwhelmed by existing workload; perceive UDL as adding "one more thing." Solutions: A. Reframe UDL as Time-Saver:

items: "UDL actually saves time in the long run because fewer students need reteaching, Tier 2 interventions, or behavior redirections."
Example: Teacher who chunks instruction spends less time managing off-task behavior → More time for actual teaching
content.title: 8.9.9 Common Challenges and Solutions

items: "You don't have to overhaul every lesson tomorrow. Pick ONE UDL principle (e.g., multiple means of representation) and add visuals to this week's lessons. Next month, add choice."
Provide UDL templates (graphic organizers, lesson plan shells) so teachers don't reinvent the wheel
Use PLC time to co-plan UDL lessons as teams
Share resources: If Grade 7 Math team creates a UDL unit, all Grade 7 teachers benefit
"I know you're busy. What if we focus on UDL for your most challenging unit this term? Let's co-plan it together so you're not alone."

content.text: Challenge 2: "I don't have enough resources—we can't afford flexible seating and technology for everyone!" Root Concern: Perception that UDL requires expensive materials. Solutions: A. Low-Cost Alternatives: ![][image23] B. Prioritize No-Cost UDL:

items: Many UDL strategies cost $0:
Chunking instruction (just pacing)
Think-Pair-Share (just structure)
Sentence frames (just write on board)
Visual schedules (just marker and whiteboard)
Message: "UDL is primarily about instructional design, not materials. You can implement strong UDL with zero budget."
Year 1: Focus on no-cost UDL (instructional strategies)
Year 2: Small budget ($100/classroom) for flexible seating additions
Year 3: Technology acquisition plan (apply for grants, fundraise)
Principals include UDL resources in annual budget proposals
Demonstrate ROI: "Last year, we reduced Tier 2 intervention costs by 20% because strong UDL prevented over-referrals."

content.text: Challenge 3: "How is UDL different from differentiation? We already do that!" Root Concern: Confusion about terminology; teachers think UDL \= what they're already doing. Solutions: A. Clarify Distinction: ![][image24] B. Affirm Current Practice:

items: "You're already differentiating, which is great! UDL is the next step—it's differentiation built into the lesson from the start so it benefits everyone, not just struggling students."
Validate effort; position UDL as evolution, not replacement
Without UDL: Teacher assigns essay. Rina (dysgraphia) receives accommodation (can type). Other students must handwrite.
With UDL: Teacher tells whole class: "You can write essay by hand OR type it." Rina types without stigma; several other students also choose typing because they prefer it.
Impact: Same accommodation, but now it's UDL—available to all, normalized.

content.text: Challenge 4: "What about students who need the structure of traditional rows and desks?" Root Concern: Fear that flexible seating \= chaos; some students need predictable environments. Solutions: A. UDL Includes Choice:

items: "UDL doesn't mean eliminate traditional desks! It means offer options. Students who prefer desks can sit at desks; students who prefer standing can stand."
Traditional seating is ONE option within UDL environment
First week of school: Practice transitions between seating areas; establish norms
Students learn: "I can choose floor cushions during independent reading, but I sit at a desk during assessments."
Structure within flexibility

content.text: C. Scaffolding for Students Needing Structure:

items: Students with autism or anxiety: Assign consistent "home base" seat; allow predictability while others have choice
Gradually introduce flexibility: Week 1-2, assigned seats; Week 3+, choice during specific activities
If flexible seating becomes disruptive (students socializing, not working), teacher adjusts:
"This week, I'm assigning seats during group work because I noticed too much off-task talk. Once we rebuild focus, we'll return to choice."
UDL is flexible—adjust based on data

Vision Statement Universal Design for Learning is not a separate program or an "add-on" for students with special needs—it is the foundation of excellent teaching for all Eduversal students. By designing learning environments, curriculum, and instruction with accessibility and flexibility from the start, we ensure that every student—regardless of language proficiency, learning difference, or background—can access rigorous Cambridge education while developing Indonesian character competencies (Profil Pelajar Pancasila). UDL strengthens our MTSS framework by making Tier 1 instruction so effective that 80-85% of students succeed without additional support. This allows us to reserve Tier 2 and Tier 3 interventions for students who genuinely need them, rather than over-identifying students whose struggles stem from poor instructional design. Implementation Roadmap for Principals Year 1: Launch (Building Awareness and Capacity) ![][image26] Year 2-3: Deepening and Sustaining

Advanced Topics: PD on UDL for assessment design, UDL for online/blended learning

content.text: Case Study 1: Tier 2 Literacy Success Student: Aisyah, Grade 3, Indonesian L1, reading below grade level Tier 1 (September-October): Teacher provides differentiated small-group reading instruction, but Aisyah continues to struggle with decoding. SST Referral (November): EASE Reading score at 25th percentile; teacher documents Tier 1 strategies attempted. Tier 2 Intervention (November-February): Aisyah receives 30 minutes daily of small-group phonics intervention using Orton-Gillingham approach. Progress monitored every 2 weeks using oral reading fluency probes. Outcome: By February, Aisyah's reading fluency improves from 45 words/minute to 78 words/minute (grade-level expectation: 80). She exits Tier 2 with monitoring plan. By June EASE, she scores at 48th percentile. Case Study 2: EAL Student Progression Student: Fajar, Grade 7, entered Eduversal school in August with limited English (Stage 2: Early Acquisition, CEFR A1-A2) Support:

items: Daily pull-out EAL lessons (45 minutes)
Push-in support during Science and Humanities (3x/week)
Modified assignments with visual scaffolds
Extended time on assessments

content.text: Case Study 3: Access Arrangements for IGCSE Student: Rafi, Grade 10, diagnosed with dyslexia and ADHD IEP Accommodations (used throughout Grade 9-10):

items: Extra time (25%) on all tests
Use of word processor for essays
Separate testing room to reduce distractions
School applies for extra time \+ word processor \+ separate room for IGCSE exams
Evidence submitted: Psychoeducational report, IEP, samples of Rafi's assessments showing use of laptop and extra time

content.text: Outcome: Cambridge approves all three accommodations. Rafi takes IGCSE exams in May with approved arrangements and earns passing grades in all subjects.

Q1: What if a parent refuses consent for SEN evaluation? A: Respect parental decision. Continue with Tier 2 interventions that do not require formal diagnosis (e.g., reading support, tutoring). Document parent refusal. Revisit conversation after 1-2 terms if student continues to struggle. Q2: Can we provide accommodations without an IEP? A: Yes, for short-term needs. Tier 2 support plans can include accommodations (e.g., preferential seating, extra time) without formal IEP. However, for Cambridge access arrangements, IEP or equivalent documentation is required. Q3: What if a student with an IEP is not making progress? A: Convene IEP review meeting (don't wait for annual review). Consider: (1) Is intervention being implemented with fidelity? (2) Do goals need to be adjusted? (3) Does student need more intensive support or different intervention? (4) Is external specialist referral needed? Q4: Can EAL students take IGCSE First Language English? A: Yes, if they have reached Stage 5 (B2+ proficiency) and teacher recommends. Many Stage 3-4 students take IGCSE English as a Second Language (ESL) instead, which is more appropriate for developing proficiency. Q5: Do students with IEPs receive modified grades? A: Depends on IEP. If student receives accommodations only (e.g., extra time), grades are not modified. If student receives curriculum modifications (e.g., reduced content, alternative assessments), grading may be modified as specified in IEP. This must be clearly documented. Cross-reference: See Section 6.3 for grading policies and Section 6.4 for standards-based grading considerations. Q6: How do we handle situations where a student's needs exceed our capacity? A: Schools should be transparent with families when specialized services (e.g., full-time 1:1 aide, intensive ABA therapy, highly specialized curriculum) are not available. Work collaboratively with family to: (1) Identify external resources, (2) Modify expectations, or (3) If necessary, recommend transfer to more specialized setting (e.g., special school, SLB) with transition support. Q7: Are there any subjects where EAL students should not receive language support? A: Foreign Language classes (e.g., Mandarin, Arabic) are taught in the target language and should not include English language support. However, EAL students may need extra scaffolding in how to approach language learning itself. Bahasa Indonesia class is often taught in Indonesian and can serve as a "break" for EAL students from English-medium instruction.

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools are committed to building truly inclusive learning communities where every student—regardless of linguistic background, learning profile, or ability—can thrive academically, socially, and emotionally. Through our Multi-Tiered System of Supports, we ensure that:

items: All students receive high-quality, differentiated instruction (Tier 1)
Students who need additional support receive timely, evidence-based interventions (Tier 2)
Students with significant needs access individualized, intensive services (Tier 3)
EAL learners develop English proficiency while maintaining their linguistic and cultural identities
Students with special educational needs receive appropriate accommodations and are valued members of our community
Gifted and talented learners are challenged and supported to reach their full potential
