content.text: Reading (Dyslexia): "By June 2026, [Student] will read grade 4-level text orally at 110 words correct per minute with 95% accuracy, as measured by DIBELS Oral Reading Fluency probes administered monthly." Math (Dyscalculia): "By June 2026, [Student] will solve 2-digit by 1-digit multiplication problems with 80% accuracy in 3 consecutive curriculum-based assessments, using visual models and manipulatives as needed." Writing (Dysgraphia): "By June 2026, [Student] will compose a 5-paragraph persuasive essay with clear thesis, supporting evidence, and conclusion, using a word processor, scoring 3 or higher on the school's writing rubric (scale 1-4)." EAL (Academic Language): "By June 2026, [Student] will use at least 8 academic vocabulary words correctly in a 250-word written response to a Science prompt, as evidenced by teacher assessment of quarterly writing samples." Behavior (ADHD): "By June 2026, [Student] will remain on-task for 20 consecutive minutes during independent work time, as measured by teacher observation using a 5-minute interval recording system, achieving this goal in 4 out of 5 observations." Social Skills (Autism): "By June 2026, [Student] will initiate peer interactions (greetings, asking to play, sharing materials) at least 3 times per day during unstructured time (recess, lunch), as documented by daily behavior log."

content.text: 504 Plan: (U.S. term, not used in Eduversal but parents may reference) A plan providing accommodations for students with disabilities under U.S. law; similar to IEP accommodations portion. Accommodations: Changes in how a student accesses curriculum/assessments without changing content or standards (e.g., extra time, assistive technology). ADHD: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder; neurodevelopmental condition affecting attention, impulse control, and/or activity level. ASD: Autism Spectrum Disorder; developmental condition affecting social communication, behavior, and sensory processing. CEFR: Common European Framework of Reference for Languages; international standard for language proficiency (A1-C2). Differentiation: Tailoring instruction to meet diverse student needs through varied content, process, product, or environment. Dyscalculia: Specific learning difference affecting mathematical reasoning and calculation. Dysgraphia: Specific learning difference affecting handwriting and written expression. Dyslexia: Specific learning difference affecting reading accuracy, fluency, and spelling despite adequate instruction. EAL: English as an Additional Language (also called ELL \- English Language Learner, or ESL \- English as a Second Language). EASE: Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam (4x per year). FAPE: Free Appropriate Public Education (U.S. term); not directly applicable in Indonesian private schools but reflects principle of appropriate education for all. IEP: Individual Education Plan; formal document outlining goals, services, and accommodations for students with identified SEN. ILP: Individual Learning Plan; similar to IEP but used for students needing Tier 3 support without formal SEN diagnosis. Modifications: Changes in what a student is expected to learn (e.g., reduced content, alternative curriculum, modified grading). MTSS: Multi-Tiered System of Supports; framework providing universal, targeted, and intensive interventions based on student need. RTI: Response to Intervention; similar to MTSS; data-driven approach to identifying and supporting struggling learners. SEN: Special Educational Needs; term for students with disabilities or learning differences requiring additional support. SpLD: Specific Learning Differences (dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, dyspraxia). SST: Student Support Team; collaborative team that reviews data and coordinates interventions for struggling students. Tier 1: Universal supports provided to all students through quality first teaching and differentiation. Tier 2: Targeted interventions for students needing additional support beyond Tier 1 (typically 15-20% of students). Tier 3: Intensive, individualized supports for students with significant needs (typically 5-10% of students). UDL: Universal Design for Learning; instructional framework providing multiple means of representation, engagement, and expression. 2E (Twice-Exceptional): Students who are both gifted and have a learning disability. Educamp: Development of Teaching Proficiency department of Eduversal (mentioned in appraisal context) EduNav: Eduversal's School Information System (SIS) and quality management platform; central repository for student data, IEPs, assessments, and communication logs. EASE: Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam; Eduversal's benchmark assessment system administered 4x per year. DTP: Development of Teaching Proficiency; Eduversal's teacher appraisal and professional growth program. Appraisal Program: Eduversal's quality assurance team that conducts school visits and reviews. Profil Pelajar Pancasila: Indonesian national character competencies framework (6 values: faith, global diversity, cooperation, independence, critical thinking, creativity).
